name: Onimaru Kunitsuna
description: Onimaru Kunitsuna is a legendary Japanese tachi (long sword) crafted by the swordsmith Awataguchi Kunitsuna during the Kamakura period (1185-1333). It is renowned as one of the Tenka Goken (Five Great Swords Under Heaven), a group of the most highly esteemed swords in Japanese history. The sword's name, 'Onimaru,' translates to 'Demon Cutter,' originating from a famous legend. The legend tells of a powerful and malevolent oni (demon) plaguing the dreams of Ashikaga Yoshimitsu, the third Ashikaga shōgun. Yoshimitsu, tormented by the oni, sought a way to vanquish it. One night, he dreamt that his sword, Onimaru, moved on its own and struck the oni, slaying it. Upon waking, Yoshimitsu found the sword stained with blood, confirming the dream's reality. Following this event, the sword was renamed Onimaru, commemorating its role in defeating the demon. The Onimaru Kunitsuna is not only celebrated for its legendary demon-slaying abilities but also for its exceptional craftsmanship. Its blade is said to possess an unparalleled sharpness and balance, making it a formidable weapon. Over the centuries, the sword has been passed down through various powerful figures and families, becoming a symbol of authority and strength. Today, the Onimaru Kunitsuna is considered a national treasure of Japan, carefully preserved and occasionally displayed, continuing to inspire awe and wonder with its rich history and legendary significance.
subtitle: Demon-Slaying Sword of the Shōgun
origin: Forged by Awataguchi Kunitsuna, empowered by slaying a demon in a dream.
currentLocation: National Treasure of Japan, location not publicly disclosed

keyMyths.title: The Demon's Nightmare
keyMyths.description: Ashikaga Yoshimitsu was plagued by nightmares caused by a powerful oni. The sword Onimaru, acting on its own, slew the demon in Yoshimitsu's dream, ending the torment.

keyMyths.title: Naming of Onimaru
keyMyths.description: After the sword's miraculous act of slaying the demon, it was renamed Onimaru, meaning 'Demon Cutter', to commemorate its role in the event. This name became synonymous with its power and legend.

sources.author: Kanzan Sato
sources.description: Discusses the history and significance of Japanese swords, including the Tenka Goken.
